summary: Gruber investigates whether Apple's web rendering framework should be spelled 'Web Kit' or 'WebKit', documenting rampant inconsistency across Apple's own documentation and developer pages. He traces the pattern of Cocoa framework naming conventions, noting that official names like Application Kit, I/O Kit, and PDF Kit all include spaces, while their abbreviated forms (like AppKit) close the gap. He concludes that 'WebKit' is likely an error born from the AppKit abbreviation convention and the influence of the closed-up 'WebCore', but ultimately just wants Apple to pick one and stick with it.
key_points: Apple's own developer pages use both 'Web Kit' and 'WebKit' nearly side by side, with different teams preferring different spellings
Cocoa naming convention consistently uses spaces in kit names (I/O Kit, PDF Kit, Search Kit), suggesting 'Web Kit' is technically correct
The closed-up 'WebKit' likely arose by analogy with 'AppKit', the accepted abbreviation of Application Kit, but unlike that case it doesn't actually abbreviate anything
The open source WebKit developers have settled on the closed-up spelling, which dominates in Google results despite being arguably incorrect
money_quotes: As a stickler for detail and consistency, this has been driving me nuts ever since the framework was announced.
Apple's inconsistency regarding whether there's a space in the name is borderline comical.

But unlike Application Kit/AppKit, which feels like a legitimate and useful abbreviation in that the short form is both easier to pronounce and to type, Web Kit/WebKit just looks like inconsistent spelling.
They're pronounced the same, and unless you have two broken thumbs, it'd be hard to argue that "WebKit" is easier to type.
Plug them into GoogleFight and "WebKit" absolutely kicks "Web Kit"'s ass; but that doesn't make it right.
My gut feeling is thus that "WebKit" is a mistake. But I don't really care which spelling Apple settles on, I just want it settled.
key_insight: Apple's failure to consistently spell its own framework's name reveals a tension between formal naming conventions and developer culture that only consistency can resolve.
